rationale_for_baseline_tier: Benign tinea corporis with mechanical excoriation — routine antifungal treatment and itch control with a secondary-infection safety-net; not urgent.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A lesion becoming hot/hard/swollen with thick pus or honey crusts, or fever
escalation_edges.communication_template: If a ring becomes hot, swollen, oozes thick pus or golden crusts, or your child develops a fever — seek urgent medical care.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Short nails, parent-applied treatment, and antihistamine to break the itch-scratch cycle.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is ringworm. Apply the antifungal cream twice daily past the red edge for a week after it clears, use the mild steroid over it for only 3 days to stop the itch, give the anti-itch medicine, keep nails short, and don't share towels. Watch for hot/pus/fever.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: GP in 7-10 days; urgent care/ED if secondary infection
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: No urgent action needed unless secondary infection/fever.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Document tinea corporis with excoriation, antifungal + brief steroid + antihistamine, and hygiene advice.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: No secondary-infection safety-net for excoriated skin.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A cellulitis develops in the excoriated skin and is not recognised.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: No treatment or itch-cycle control, so scratching/bleeding continues.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cannot KOH-scrape remotely, but the annular scaly advancing border is characteristic.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat as tinea corporis with antifungal + itch control + hygiene and safety-net for infection.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Over-treating as cellulitis or failing to control the itch.
